regions.label: Plato and Aristotle
regions.description: The two central figures - Plato points upward to the realm of Forms, Aristotle gestures toward the earth

regions.label: Euclid
regions.description: Bent over a slate, demonstrating geometry to students gathered around him

regions.label: Heraclitus
regions.description: The brooding figure sitting alone on the steps, often said to be modeled after Michelangelo

regions.label: Diogenes
regions.description: Sprawled casually on the steps, embodying his rejection of material comfort

regions.label: Pythagoras
regions.description: Writing in a large book, surrounded by students eager to learn his theories of harmony

beats.narration: One painting. Fifty philosophers. Two thousand years of human thought, frozen in a single room.
At the center: the argument that started everything. Plato points up — truth is abstract. Aristotle pushes down — truth is here, in the world.
Sitting alone, ignoring everyone. This is Heraclitus — or rather, Michelangelo. Raphael painted his rival into the scene as a tribute.
And this guy? Diogenes. Lying on the steps like he owns the place. He once told Alexander the Great to move — he was blocking the sun.
Pythagoras, deep in his notebook. The man who believed the universe was built on numbers and harmony.
Euclid draws a perfect circle on his slate. His students lean in. Geometry — the language of the cosmos.
Every figure here shaped the world. And Raphael put them all in one room — because great ideas never exist alone.
